text: Lecture	  36	  –	  Somatosensory	  Physiology	   	   Learning	  outcomes	  	   •	  Describe	  variety	  of	  somatosensory	  receptors	  and	  the	  stimuli	  they	  detect	   •	  Describe	  receptive	  fields	  of	  somatosensory	  neurons	  and	  relationship	  to	  tactile	  acuity	   •	  Explain	  functional	  organization	  of	  ascending	  pathways	  and	  somatosensory	  cortex	   	   The	  somatic	  senses	  are	  classified	  into	  3	  types:	  mechanoreceptive,	  thermoreceptive	  and	  pain	  receptive	   	   Skin	  Receptors	   1. Bare	  nerve	  endings	  (Slow	  adapting	  aka	  tonic	  receptors)	   a. Pain,	  temperature	   2. Nerve	  ends	  with	  accessory	  structures	   a. eg	  pacinian,	  merkel	  endings,	  Meissner’s	   corpuscles,	  Hair	  endings,	  Ruffini	  ending	  (Slow	   and	  Fast	  adapting	  response)	   i. Respond	  to	  mechanical	  energy	   (pressure,	  light	  touch,	  vibration)	   Pacinian	  corpuscles	  and	  hair	  endings	  are	  very	  fast	  adapting,	   Ruffini	  endings	  and	  merkels	  discs	  are	  slow	  adapting.	   	   Modalities	  &	  Receptors	   • Different	  modalities	  carried	  by	  different	  fibre	  groups	   • Mechanosense	  –	  much	  overlap	  between	  the	  classifications	  of	  them	   o Group	  Aα,	  Aβ	  overlap	  with	  1	  and	  2	   o All	  large	  diameter	  myelinated	  fibres	   • Pain	  and	  temperature	  -­‐>	  Need	  to	  know.	   o Group	  A	  delta:	  small	  diameter	  myelinated	  fibres	   o Group	  C:	  small	  diameter	  UNmyelinated	  fibres	   The	  5	  types	  of	  sensory	  receptors	  are	  mechanoreceptors,	  thermoreceptors,	  nociceptors,	  electromagnetic	  receptors	   (ie	  light)	  and	  chemoreceptors	  (the	  last	  two	  are	  associated	  with	  the	  special	  senses)	   	   Spatial	  Acuity	  (See	  L32	  -­‐	  Receptive	  fields	  of	  neurons)	   • Two	  point	  discrimination	  threshold:	   o The	  distance	  apart	  two	  points	  must	  be	  before	  we	  can	  differentiate	  them	  as	  two	  points,	  rather	   than	  one.	   o Fingers,	  hand	  etc	  have	  very	  low	  threshold	   o Back	  etc	  have	  higher	  threshold	   o Dependent	  on	  size	  of	  receptive	  fields,	  number	  of	  nerves,	  connectivity	  of	  the	  pathway	  and	  lateral	   inhibition	  –	  	  (See	  L32	  -­‐	  Receptive	  fields	  of	  neurons)	   	   Mechanosensitive	  Sensory	  afferents	   • Provide	  information	  to	  higher	  centres	  for	  further	  processing/finer	  control	   • Include	  tactile	  (touch,	  pressure,	  vibration	  and	  tickle	  senses	  –	  see	  above	  in	  ‘skin	  receptors’),	  position	   (proprioception)	  and	  deep	  sensations	  –	  from	  deep	  tissues	  (bone,	  joints)	   	   Joint	  (proprioception)	   • Mechanosensitive	  receptors	   • Angle	  of	  joint,	  stretch,	  rate	  of	  change,	  	   o different	  receptors	  activate	  at	  different	  angles	   • slow	  adapting	  –	  so	  you	  always	  know	  where	  your	  joints	  are	   	   	   	   	   Muscle	  (deep	  sensations)	   • Specialised	  intrafusal	  receptors	  (muscle	  spindle)	  –	  respond	  to	  muscle	  length	  changes	   • Golgi	  tendon	  organ	  –	  muscle	  tension	   o Activated	  by	  stretch,	  causes	  inhibitive	  reflex	  arc	  that	  inhibits	  muscle	  fibres	  causing	  relaxation	  of	   muscle	  in	  response	  to	  very	  large	  force	  placed	  on	  that	  arm	   • slowly	  adapting	   • provide	  reflex	  connections	  to	   motor	  neurons	   • provide	  proprioceptive	  input	  to	   higher	  centres	  (limb	  position	   sense)	   Large	  fibre	  neuropathy	   • Loss	  of	  joint	  and	  muscle	  sensory	   input	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Thermo-­‐receptive	   • Bare	  nerve	  endings	   Cold	  receptor	   • Firing	  rate	  increase	  in	  response	  to	  falls	  in	  temp	  and	  vice	   versa	   • At	  very	  high	  temperatures	  (paradoxical	  cold)	   o Cold	  nerve’s	  get	  damaged	  causing	  stimuli	   	   Warm	  receptor	   • Firing	  rate	  increase	  in	  response	  to	  rises	  in	  temp	  and	  vice	   versa	   • 38	  degrees,	  firing	  rate	  is	  max.	   	   Interaction	  of	  the	  2	  receptor	  types	  gives	  the	  overall	  sense	  of	   temperature.	  	   	   Nociceptors	   • Respond	  to	  pain/potentially	  damaging	  stimuli	   o Have	  v.	  high	  threshold	   • Do	  they	  adapt	  to	  the	  pain?	  No,	  they	  are	  very	  slowly	   adapting	  receptors,	  and	  pain	  usually	  isn’t	  applied	  for	   long	  enough	  (ie	  days)	   	   Pathways	  for	  Sensory	  Stimuli	  	   Dorsal	  Column-­‐Medial	  Lemnisci	  Pathway	   o Dorsal	  column	  refers	  to	  pathway	  in	  the	  spinal	  cord	  (primary	  neurons)	   o Medial	  lemnisci	  is	  a	  tract	  from	  the	  medulla	  to	  thalamus	  (secondary	  neurons)	   o Connecting	  these	  are	  the	  gracile	  and	  cuneate	  nuclei	  of	  medulla.	   • Carries	  Fine	  Tactile	  and	  Proprioceptive	   • Fibres	  of	  pathway	  are	  arranged	  such	  that	  distal	  sensations	  are	  in	  the	  medial	  part	  and	  proximal	  nerve	   fibres	  get	  added	  on	  the	  lateral	  portion.	   o Nucleus	  gracilis	  –lower	  limb	   o Nucleus	  cuneatus	  –	  upper	  limb	   • High	  degree	  of	  spatial	  orientation,	  with	  respect	  to	  origin	  on	  surface	  of	  body	  of	  the	  nerve	  fibres	   • Faster	  rate	  of	  transmission	  (larger	  diameter	  and	  myelinated)	   7 Golgi tendon organs detect muscle force Muscle receptors •Muscle spindle afferents •Golgi tendon organs •Provide reflex connections to motor neurons •Provide proprioceptive input to higher centres-limb position sense 8 From Kandel et al Large fibre neuropathy-loss of joint and muscle sensory input Thermoreceptors-separate warm and cold receptors 	   	   Spinothalamic	  (ventrolateral)	  Pathway	   • Carries	  thermo	  sensitive,	  nociceptive,	  crude	  touch	  and	  pressure.	   o Including	  tickle	  and	  itch	  sensations	   • Smaller	  degree	  of	  spatial	  orientation,	  with	  respect	  to	  origin	  of	  nerve	  fibres	  on	  surface	   • Slower	  rate	  of	  transmission	  (smaller	  diameter	  and	  unmyelinated)	   • Spatial	  sensation	  is	  relayed	  (with	  dorsal	  column	  signals)	  to	  the	  somatosensory	  area	  1	  via	  thalamus.	   • Thermo	  sensitive	  signals	  go	  to	  thalamus	   • Pain	  signals	  are	  predominantly	  relayed	  to	  thalamus	  (some	  go	  to	  reticular	  formation)	   	   	   	   	   From	  Wikipedia:	   The	  gracile	  nucleus	  and	  fasciculus	  carry	  epicritic,	  kinesthetic,	  and	  conscious	  proprioceptive	  information	  from	  the	  lower	   part	  of	  the	  body	  (below	  the	  level	  of	  T6	  in	  the	  spinal	  cord).	  The	  counterpart	  to	  the	  gracile	  nucleus	  and	  fasciculus	  is	   the	  cuneate	  nucleus	  and	  fasciculus,	  which	  carries	  the	  same	  type	  of	  information,	  but	  from	  the	  upper	  body	  (above	  T6,	   excepting	  the	  face	  and	  ear	  -­‐	  the	  information	  from	  the	  face	  and	  ear	  is	  carried	  by	  the	  principal	  sensory	  nucleus	  of	  trigeminal	   nerve).	   	   80 NEUROPHYSIOLOGY FIGURE 2.29 SOMESTHETIC SYSTEM OF THE BODY• Pain, temperature, and pressure sensations below the head ulti- mately are conveyed to the primary somatosensory cortex (post- central gyrus) by the anterolateral system (spinothalamic and spin- oreticular tracts). The fasciculus gracilis and cuneatus of the spinal lemniscal system convey proprioceptive, vibratory, and tactile sen- sations to the thalamus (ventral posterolateral nucleus), whereas the lateral cervical system mediates some touch, vibratory, and proprioceptive sensations (blue and purple lines show these dual pathways). Ultimately, these ﬁbers ascend as parallel pathways to the thalamus, synapse, and ascend to the cortex. Cerebral cortex: postcentral gyrus Posterior limb of internal capsule Ventral posterolateral (VPL) nucleus of thalamus Medial lemniscus Gracile nucleus Cuneate nucleus Fasciculus gracilis Fasciculus cuneatus Dorsal (posterior) spinal root ganglion Proprioception, position Large myelinated fibers Touch, pressure, vibration Pain, temperature Small myelinated and unmyelin- ated fibers Lateral cervical nucleus Spinocervical tract Lumbar part of spinal cord Ventral (anterior) spinothalamic tract: touch, pressure Lateral spinothalamic tract: pain, temperature Cervical part of spinal cord Reticular formation Lower part of medulla oblongata Spinothalamic tract Mesencephalon (cerebral peduncles) Sensory Pathways: I © Organisation	  of	  somatosensory	  Cortex	   Somatosensory	  cortex	  =	  area	  of	  cerebral	  cortex	  to	  which	  the	  sensory	  signals	  are	  projected	   	   Primary	  Somatosensory	  Cortex	   • S1	   o On	  the	  post	  central	  gyrus	   o Subdivisions	  that	  process	  different	  modalities	  of	  sensation	  (Brodmanns	  areas):	    Areas	  3a	  –	  Input	  from	  deep	  muscle	  tissue	  particularly,	  muscle	  spindle	  afferent	    Areas	  3b	  –	  Input	  from	  slowly	  and	  rapidly	  adapting	  skin	  receptors	   • Encode	  the	  features/shape	  of	  stimuli	  on	  the	  skin.	    Area	  1	  –	  input	  from	  skin	  (rapidly	  adapting	  receptors)	    Area	  2	  –	  stimulated	  by	  movement	  across	  the	  skin	  (direction	  sensitive)	  more	  complex	   stimuli,	  by	  processing	  the	  stimulus	  of	  3a/3b.	  also	  input	  from	  deep	  tissues	  (pressure	  and	   joint	  position)	    Hierarchical	  processing	  by	  these	  areas	  1	  and	  2	  mainly,	  in	  addition	  to	  parallel	  processing	   	   Secondary	  Somatosensory	  Cortex	   • S2	  –	  gets	  bilateral	  information	  via	  intracortical	  communications	   o Face,	  arm	  and	  leg	  are	  represented	  in	  this	  region	   	   Parietal	  Cortex	   • Takes	  into	  account	  emotions,	  experience	  etc	   	   	   Somatotopic	  map	   • Each	  cortical	  area	  of	  the	  primary	  somatosensory	  cortex	  	  eg	  3a	  etc.	  have	  their	  own	  somatotopic	  map.	   	   An	  attempt	  at	  a	  Summary	   	   • So	  sensory	  innervations	  coming	  in	  can	  be	  processed	  by	  different	  areas	  of	  the	  somatosensory	  cortex	  (eg	   3a)	  depending	  on	  the	  type	  of	  sensation/stimuli	  received.	  These	  areas	  provides	  different	  grades	  of	   processing	  eg	  parallel	  or	  hierarchal.	  Within	  each	  area	  though	  there	  must	  be	  the	  ability	  to	  process	  the	   signals	  from	  different	  parts	  of	  the	  body	  (somatostopic	  map)	  =	  increasingly	  complex	  processing	   • Parallel	  is	  where	  information	  goes	  to	  the	  thalamus	  first	  and	  is	  then	  sent	  to	  different	  cortical	  areas	  for	   processing	   • Hierarchical	  is	  where	  the	  areas	  pass	  stimuli	  between	  each	  other	  (intracortical),	  thus	  allowing	  greater	   processing	  of	  some	  types	  of	  stimuli	  eg	  hand?	   • Somatic	  sensory	  area	  one	  	   o Anterior	  and	  middle	  of	  postcentral	  gyrus	  =	  tactile	  (1,	  3b)	   o Posterior	  of	  area	  1	  =	  joint	  receptors	  and	  pressure	  (2)	   o Extreme	  anterior	  =	  muscle	  spindle	  (3a)
